exemplar.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Maintains a sustained focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay.
Provides a range of relevant, well-chosen examples from History and the Human Sciences (Ottoman historiography, Holocaust studies, Stanford Prison Experiment, Harlowâs monkeys).
Demonstrates clear organization and logical transitions, particularly in moving from History to the Human Sciences.
Highlights the ethical dimension of evidence evaluation with convincing illustrative cases.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive and asserts rather than unpacks key TOK concepts (e.g. bias, justification, perspectives).
Limited use of TOK terminology and insufficient depth in evaluating alternative viewpoints.
Conceptual imprecision (e.g. introducing âcognitive integrityâ without definition).
Conclusion is generic and fails to integrate counterarguments or reflect on broader knowledge-community implications.

annotations.comment: âCognitive integrityâ is introduced without clarification, which weakens conceptual precision and leaves the reader uncertain of its TOK relevance.
The introduction asserts the directive but does not explicitly formulate a TOK knowledge question, limiting clarity on the precise scope of inquiry.
The synthesis of historical and human sciences cases effectively underlines the shared ethical responsibility across disciplines.
The conclusion lacks explicit reflection on alternative perspectives or potential counterarguments, missing a deeper TOK evaluation.
The student maintains a focused engagement with the prescribed title throughout the essay, framing the central ethical directive clearly as the basis for discussion.
The term âconfirmation biasâ is mentioned without definition or link to the TOK concept of bias, reducing the analytical precision of the discussion.
Early recognition of the ethical stakes in evidence evaluation demonstrates awareness of the topicâs significance in both history and human sciences.
Use of Ottoman historiography effectively illustrates how access to new evidence can reshape historical narratives, aligning with the titleâs focus.
Discussion of colonial viewpoints and Orientalism is insightful but needs deeper evaluation of their ethical implications within historical methodology.
The paragraph on limitations of earlier historians remains descriptive and lacks analysis of how those constraints affect knowledge justification.
The narrative remains largely descriptive; it asserts rather than examines how ethical obligations arise from shifts in available Holocaust evidence.
References to survivor testimonies lack discussion of reliability and validity, omitting how these factors impact the ethical directive in history.
The discussion of digital archives and multidisciplinary approaches in Holocaust studies highlights important methodological progress.
The transition from history to human sciences is well signposted, maintaining logical structure and coherence in the essayâs development.
The account of the Stanford Prison Experiment omits engagement with ethical frameworks in TOK, such as deontological versus consequentialist reasoning.
Ethical considerations for participant protection in the Stanford Prison Experiment are not examined in relation to the essayâs central directive.
Identification of methodological flaws in the Stanford Prison Experiment demonstrates a critical approach to evidence evaluation.
There is no explicit discussion of how the notion of justification operates in historical knowledge claims, reducing the depth of TOK analysis.
The Holocaust example clearly links evidence availability to evolving interpretations, demonstrating awareness of changing knowledge frameworks.
The student fails to discuss how researcher roles introduce biases, lacking use of TOK terminology such as âperspectivesâ or âjustification.â
Claiming that âoversimplified conclusionsâ arise from ignoring evidence, the essay does not unpack criteria by which simplicity undermines validity.
The final call for âdeliberate effort, critical thinking, and ethical behaviorâ underscores the essayâs normative dimension and encourages future inquiry.
The essay does not connect these methodological critiques back to the core ethical directive or to broader knowledge concerns explicitly.
The Harlow monkey experiment example enriches the argument by showing cross-disciplinary parallels in handling contradictory data.
The concluding remarks are generic and do not address the implications of the directive for knowledge communities or policymaking.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
